originalTitle: Trump announces new tariffs on generic drugs to take effect in 2028 – US politics live
summary: President says 100% tariff would be imposed on generic drugs made overseas after 1 August 2028 ‘for a one year period of time, and 200% thereafter’
content: As Donald Trump announces new tariffs daily, leading one sober trade expert to observe, “Tariff Crazytown is officially BACK”, Democratic lawmakers are lining up to promise to repeal the Trump tariffs as soon as they regain control of Congress. “Democrats will flip the House. And on Day 1 of next term, Democrats will introduce legislation to repeal trump’s disastrous tariffs. And we will pass it,” congressman Ted Lieu, a California Democrat, wrote after Trump imposed 50% tariffs on a range of goods from Canada on Monday, citing Section 338 of the disastrous Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930 as his legal authority. “Donald Trump has abused every trade authority at his disposal, and he is now excavating a law from the Great Depression to once again impose vast, unilateral tariffs on products from one of our closest allies and trading partners,” senator Ron Wyden, an Oregon Democrat, said in a statement. “This is yet another shakedown that will raise the cost-of-living for Americans, their families, and small businesses across the country. Trump cannot be trusted with tariff authority, and I will be introducing a bill very soon to rein him in and put Congress back in the driver’s seat.” In a Substack column republished by the Toronto Star on Tuesday, the Nobel-winning economist Paul Krugman wrote: The Trade Act of 1930, generally known as the Smoot-Hawley tariff, lost its standing as the worst trade policy action in US history when Donald Trump imposed his “Liberation Day” tariffs in April 2025. But Smoot-Hawley was widely recognized as a terrible mistake soon after its passage, as it provoked widespread retaliation and contributed to the downward spiral of world trade that accompanied and reinforced the Great Depression. … Yesterday the Trump administration invoked an obscure and never-before-used provision of Smoot-Hawley — Section 338 — to impose 50 percent tariffs on a wide range of Canadian goods. … Whatever the real motivation for these new tariffs, they are almost surely illegal. They are definitely a violation of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement, a revision of NAFTA negotiated and signed by Trump himself during his first term … So these latest actions confirm what most people around the world, from the European Commission to Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard, have already figured out: a deal with Donald Trump lasts only until he feels like breaking it. Donald Trump announced a new package of tariffs on Tuesday, this time on generic drugs made overseas, to begin in 2028. The announcement, in a social media post, came just three months after the president issued a proclamation saying that generic pharmaceuticals “are not subject to tariffs at this time” but that decision “will be reassessed in one year.” Trump’s latest head-spinning reversal on tariff policy means, he said, that generic drugs will continue to be imported tariff-free until 1 August 2028, when a 100% tariff would be imposed “for a one year period of time, and 200% thereafter.” The goal of the policy, Trump said, was to coerce drug makers into building plants in the United States. Trump did not state what legal authority he was claiming to impose future tariffs on generic drugs, particularly the projected 200% tariff which would come into effect in August 2029, six months after the end of his final term. The US-Israeli war on Iran has cost the Pentagon $37.5bn so far, defense secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday. At the Senate hearing, which has now concluded, senator Dick Durbin, an Illinois Democrat, asked Hegseth to explain why the Pentagon was asking Congress for an additional $67bn to pay for the war Trump started on 28 February when “the Department of Defense still has $75bn in unobligated funding from last year’s reconciliation package”. “That 75,” Hegseth replied, “there’s plans and designs on that part of the rebuild of our military which had to be undertaken.” Hegseth then launched into a deeply partisan attack on his predecessor, Lloyd Austin, the retired army general, for supposedly pursuing “woke priorities” instead of military readiness. “I don’t know what Lloyd Austin did for four years, I really don’t,” he went on. “The amount of neglect, the amount of stale thinking and bureaucracy proceed staggering when we took over.” “Let me interrupt your speech for a minute, Mr Secretary,” Durbin cut in. “You estimated at one point that the cost of the war in Iran was $29bn, but then added a caveat that there was a replacement of munitions, for example. Do you have a new estimate about what the war has cost us so far in this the fifth month of the first year of this war in Iran?” the senator asked. “Senator, the estimate we have, as of today, is $37.5bn,” Hegseth answered. Pressed further by Durbin, Hegseth said that was the estimated cost of the war on Iran through the end of the US government’s financial year on 30 September. The White House called on Tuesday for the House and Senate to pass the Republican SAVE America Act, which would require proof of citizenship to register to vote, and photo ID to cast a ballot, after New Jersey’s governor, Mikie Sherrill, revealed that a computer software error in the state’s automatic voter registration system for licensed drivers mistakenly registered about 6,600 people who identified themselves as non-US citizens in 2023 and 2024. According to Sherrill, who was not in office at the time, “fewer than 400 individuals who were newly registered for the first time because of the error voted. They were registered as Democrats, Republicans and Unaffiliated voters and were scattered across the state.” David Becker, a voting rights expert who leads the nonprofit Center for Election Innovation & Research, put the size of the glitch into context in a statement sent to reporters. “While administrative errors such as New Jersey’s do infrequently occur, they are very rare and affect a very small fraction of voters. chineseTitle: 特朗普宣布对仿制药征收新关税将于 2028 年生效——美国政治直播
chineseSummary: 总统表示，2028 年 8 月 1 日后将对海外生产的仿制药征收 100% 的关税，为期一年，此后征收 200% 的关税
chineseContent: 随着唐纳德·特朗普每天宣布新的关税，一位清醒的贸易专家观察到，“关税疯狂城正式回归”，民主党议员们正排队承诺一旦重新控制国会就废除特朗普的关税。 “民主党将推翻众议院。在下个任期的第一天，民主党将提出废除特朗普灾难性关税的立法。我们会通过它，”周一特朗普对一系列加拿大商品征收 50% 的关税后，加利福尼亚州民主党众议员特德·刘 (Ted Lieu) 写道，并援引了灾难性的 1930 年斯穆特-霍利关税法案第 338 条作为其合法权力。俄勒冈州民主党参议员罗恩·怀登在一份声明中表示：“唐纳德·特朗普滥用了他掌握的所有贸易权力，他现在正在挖掘大萧条时期的一项法律，再次对我们最亲密的盟友和贸易伙伴之一的产品征收巨额单方面关税。” “这又是一次整顿，将提高美国人、他们的家庭和全国小企业的生活成本。特朗普的关税权力不能被信任，我将很快提出一项法案来控制他，让国会重新掌握主导权。”周二，诺贝尔经济学奖获得者保罗·克鲁格曼 (Paul Krugman) 在多伦多星报重新发表的 Substack 专栏中写道：当唐纳德·特朗普 (Donald Trump) 在 2025 年 4 月实施“解放日”关税时，1930 年贸易法案（通常被称为斯穆特霍利关税）失去了美国历史上最糟糕的贸易政策行动的地位。但斯穆特霍利法案通过后不久就被广泛认为是一个可怕的错误，因为它引发了广泛的报复，并导致了经济下行。伴随并强化大萧条的世界贸易螺旋式上升。 ......昨天，特朗普政府援引了一项模糊的规定
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
